questions.question: Which American civil rights leader was assassinated in Memphis, Tennessee on April 4, 1968?
questions.explanation: Martin Luther King Jr. was shot and killed on April 4, 1968, at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, Tennessee, where he had come to support striking sanitation workers. James Earl Ray was convicted of the murder. King's assassination triggered riots in over 100 American cities. The following day, Robert F. Kennedy was shot and killed in Los Angeles. 1968 was one of the most traumatic years in American history.
questions.memory_hook: King assassinated April 4, 1968, Memphis. Lorraine Motel. He was there for a sanitation workers' strike — not a major speech, just labor solidarity. RFK was shot 2 months later. MLK was 39. The greatest advocate for nonviolence was killed by violence.

questions.question: The 'Iron Curtain,' a term used to describe the division between communist and non-communist Europe during the Cold War, was coined by which leader?
questions.explanation: Winston Churchill coined the phrase 'Iron Curtain' in a speech at Westminster College in Fulton, Missouri on March 5, 1946: 'From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Continent.' Churchill was no longer Prime Minister when he gave the speech (he had been voted out in 1945), but it became one of the defining phrases of the Cold War.
questions.memory_hook: Iron Curtain = Churchill, 1946. He wasn't even PM anymore — just an elder statesman giving a speech in Missouri. The phrase crystallized the Cold War division for the public. The Soviet response: Churchill was a warmonger. Which is what a country building an empire says to someone describing it.

questions.question: The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in which year?
questions.explanation: The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April 26, 1986, at the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant in Pripyat, Soviet Ukraine (now Ukraine). It is considered the worst nuclear accident in history. Reactor 4 exploded during a safety test, releasing radiation across Europe. The surrounding area remains largely uninhabited. The Soviet government's initial cover-up contributed to growing public distrust that helped accelerate the USSR's collapse.
questions.memory_hook: Chernobyl = April 26, 1986. The Soviet government tried to cover it up. Swedish radiation sensors detected the fallout and forced disclosure. The disaster contributed to the fall of the Soviet Union. The exclusion zone is now an accidental wildlife sanctuary — nature reclaiming a radioactive ghost town.

questions.question: What is the term for a score of zero in a cricket innings?
questions.explanation: A 'duck' in cricket refers to a batsman scoring zero runs before being dismissed. The term comes from 'duck's egg' for the oval shape of the zero. Getting out for zero on the first ball is called a 'golden duck,' and zero off a run-out before facing a ball is a 'diamond duck.' Being dismissed for a duck is considered particularly embarrassing, especially for established batters.
questions.memory_hook: Duck = zero in cricket. From 'duck's egg' (oval like a zero). Golden duck = out for zero on the first ball. Getting a duck is cricket's version of a strikeout — embarrassing, especially for a star batter. Donald Bradman, arguably cricket's greatest player, was out for a duck on his final Test innings.

questions.question: In which combat sport would competitors attempt to throw or pin each other to the mat?
questions.explanation: Judo is a Japanese martial art and Olympic sport where competitors attempt to throw opponents to the ground or pin them. 'Judo' means 'gentle way' in Japanese — the philosophy is to use the opponent's force against them rather than meeting force with force. It was developed by Jigoro Kano in 1882 and became an Olympic sport in 1964 (men) and 1992 (women). Judokas are ranked by belt color, from white to black.
questions.memory_hook: Judo = throw or pin. 'Gentle way' — use your opponent's force against them. Invented by Jigoro Kano in 1882 in Japan. Olympic since 1964. The philosophy of minimum effort for maximum effect influenced jiu-jitsu and other grappling arts.

questions.question: 'Like a Rolling Stone' by Bob Dylan was released in 1965. At 6 minutes long, it was controversial for its length. Who called it the greatest song ever written?
questions.explanation: Rolling Stone magazine ranked 'Like a Rolling Stone' as the greatest song of all time in their 2004 list of the 500 Greatest Songs. The song was revolutionary — at 6 minutes, it was twice the length of typical pop songs. Radio stations refused to play it at first. Its opening snare drum hit is one of the most recognizable sounds in rock history.
questions.memory_hook: 'Like a Rolling Stone' = Dylan's greatest. Rolling Stone magazine #1 song of all time. 6 minutes long — radio stations refused to play it. The opening snare hit is immediately recognizable. A song about someone losing everything who has to start from scratch. Dylan said it came from a long piece of writing, 'like a piece of vomit.'

questions.question: Roy Lichtenstein's Pop Art paintings of the early 1960s — featuring comic-book panels enlarged to gallery scale — were both celebrated and attacked on opposite grounds. What was the controversy, and why does it persist?
options: Lichtenstein was celebrated for elevating commercial art to fine art status, but attacked by feminists because all his enlarged comic panels depicted women in distress — critics argued he was reinforcing rather than critiquing those stereotypes

Lichtenstein was celebrated by the art establishment for his technical mastery, but attacked by critics who noted his work was entirely self-created — he didn't actually copy comics, he invented fictional panels that mimicked the style

Lichtenstein was celebrated for bringing comic book aesthetics into galleries, but attacked because he copied actual panels from real comic artists nearly verbatim without credit, compensation, or permission — while making millions

Lichtenstein was both celebrated and attacked by the same critics — some of whom argued his work was brilliant irony, while others in the same reviews called it lazy appropriation — the ambiguity was intentional
questions.answer: Lichtenstein was celebrated for bringing comic book aesthetics into galleries, but attacked because he copied actual panels from real comic artists nearly verbatim without credit, compensation, or permission — while making millions
questions.explanation: Art historian David Barsalou spent decades documenting Lichtenstein's source material, showing that paintings like 'Whaam!' (1963) and 'Drowning Girl' (1963) were extremely close copies of specific panels by artists including Russ Heath, Tony Abruzzo, and others. The original artists — working anonymously for publishers on work-for-hire contracts — received nothing while Lichtenstein's versions sold for tens of millions. 'Whaam!' sold for $43 million in 2011. Lichtenstein argued he transformed the work through scale, color, and context. The debate touches on appropriation, copyright (the panels were published in the 1950s-60s), and the art world's hierarchy that valued Lichtenstein while ignoring his sources.
questions.memory_hook: Lichtenstein's 'Whaam!' sold for $43 million. The artists he copied — Russ Heath, Tony Abruzzo, others — received nothing; they'd signed work-for-hire contracts. David Barsalou spent decades documenting how closely the paintings matched the originals. Lichtenstein said 'transformation' made it art. The original comic artists mostly went uncredited for decades.

questions.question: The 2021 film 'The Power of the Dog' was directed by which New Zealand filmmaker?
questions.explanation: Jane Campion directed The Power of the Dog (2021), based on Thomas Savage's 1967 novel. The film starred Benedict Cumberbatch, Kirsten Dunst, Jesse Plemons, and Kodi Smit-McPhee. Campion won the Academy Award for Best Director — the third woman ever to win and the second to be nominated twice. She previously won for The Piano (1993) screenplay.
questions.memory_hook: Jane Campion = Power of the Dog. Third woman to win Best Director Oscar. Benedict Cumberbatch plays a menacing, repressed rancher. Set in Montana, filmed in New Zealand — the landscape is actually New Zealand standing in for the American West.

questions.question: The Suez Canal, completed in 1869, dramatically shortened the sea route between Europe and Asia by connecting the Mediterranean Sea to which body of water?
questions.explanation: The Suez Canal connects Port Said on the Mediterranean to Suez on the Red Sea, cutting through the Isthmus of Suez in Egypt. Before the canal, ships had to sail around the southern tip of Africa. The canal reduced the voyage from London to Mumbai by over 7,000 km. Egypt nationalized it in 1956, triggering the Suez Crisis.
questions.memory_hook: Suez Canal: Mediterranean → Red Sea. Skips around Africa. Egypt, 1869.

questions.question: What is the term for the role or position a species plays in its ecosystem?
questions.explanation: An ecological niche describes the role and position of a species within its ecosystem — what it eats, what eats it, when and where it is active, and how it affects its environment. The concept explains how multiple species can coexist in the same habitat without directly competing. If two species occupy the same niche, one typically outcompetes the other — this is called competitive exclusion.
questions.memory_hook: Niche = a species' role in the ecosystem. Not where it lives (habitat) but what it does there. Two species can share a habitat but not the same niche — if they do, one wins and one loses. Evolution constantly creates new niches.

questions.question: Mount Whitney, the highest peak in the contiguous United States at 4,421 meters, is located in which western state?
questions.explanation: Mount Whitney is located in the Sierra Nevada mountain range in eastern California, on the boundary between Tulare and Inyo Counties. It is just 135 km from Death Valley — which at -86 meters below sea level is the lowest point in North America. Mount Whitney sits in Sequoia National Park / John Muir Wilderness. The trail to its summit is one of the most popular day hikes in the US but requires altitude acclimation due to oxygen reduction at its peak.
questions.memory_hook: Mount Whitney: 4,421m, highest in the contiguous US, in California's Sierra Nevada. Just 135 km from Death Valley (lowest point in N. America). The extreme juxtaposition of highest and lowest in the same region. A popular summit hike, but at 4,421m the altitude requires preparation.

questions.question: What is an ecological niche, and why can two species rarely occupy the exact same niche in an ecosystem?
options: A physical location where a species lives
The role a species plays in its ecosystem including what it eats and what eats it, which cannot be shared identically
The evolutionary history of a species
The geographic range a species occupies
questions.answer: The role a species plays in its ecosystem including what it eats and what eats it, which cannot be shared identically
questions.explanation: An ecological niche is the functional role a species plays in its ecosystem — its diet, habitat preferences, activity timing, predators, and behaviors. The competitive exclusion principle (Gause's Law) states that two species competing for identical resources in the same niche cannot coexist indefinitely — one will outcompete and replace the other. Species coexist by occupying slightly different niches (niche differentiation). Darwin's finches on the Galápagos evolved different beak shapes to exploit different food sources and avoid direct competition.
questions.memory_hook: Ecological niche = a species' role (what it eats, when active, what eats it). Two species can't occupy identical niches — one wins (competitive exclusion). Darwin's finches split into 15+ species to fill different niches. Niche differentiation is why biodiversity doesn't collapse into one dominant species.
